title: Welsh cawl
subtitle: Slow-cooked lamb, leek, and root vegetable broth from Wales
excerpt: Wales's national dish is a one-pot braise of lamb neck or shoulder with leeks, carrots, swede, and potatoes, cooked long enough that the meat becomes tender and the broth turns rich. Traditionally served over two days, with the broth on day one and the meat on day two.
sourceNotes: Welsh cawl is a dish with deep regional roots documented in Welsh agricultural and household records from the medieval period onward. The leek-and-lamb version became synonymous with Welsh national identity from the eighteenth century. No single canonical published source; the method follows the cross-referenced consensus of Welsh cookery writers including Bobby Freeman, First Catch Your Peacock (1980, revised 1996).

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the broth and meat separately if possible. Reheat gently; the vegetables will soften further but the flavour holds.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well. The broth improves on the second day once the fat has set and can be lifted off.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cook the day before. Refrigerate overnight, lift the fat from the surface, and reheat before serving.

content.text: Cawl is both a soup and a stew — or, in the traditional two-day method, a soup followed by a stew. On the first day, the broth is ladled into bowls and eaten with bread, sometimes with a lump of caerphilly cheese on the side. On the second day, the meat and vegetables are lifted out and served as a main course. This is good farmhouse economy: one pot of ingredients, two meals.

Lamb neck is the right cut: it has bones for flavour and enough connective tissue to cook down into tender, yielding meat over two and a half hours. The leeks go in late, so they stay green and distinct rather than dissolving into the broth. Add the potatoes for the final 30 minutes; they should be just cooked through, not falling apart.

Put the lamb in a large pot and cover with 2 litres of cold water. Add the {{onion}} onion, salt, and pepper. Bring slowly to a simmer, skimming off any grey foam that rises. Reduce the heat and cook at a gentle simmer for 90 minutes.

Add the {{carrot}} carrots and {{swede}} swede to the pot and cook for a further 30 minutes. Add the {{potato}} potatoes and cook for 30 minutes more, until the potatoes are tender when pierced with a knife.

Add the {{leek}} leeks and cook for 10 minutes. Taste the broth and adjust the salt. Stir in the {{parsley-flat}} of parsley. Serve immediately, or allow to cool and refrigerate overnight — the following day the fat will have set on the surface and can be lifted off before reheating.

items.symptom: Broth is fatty and rich in an unpleasant way
items.cause: Lamb neck releases considerable fat during cooking
items.fix: Cook the cawl the day before, refrigerate overnight, and lift the solidified fat from the surface before reheating

items.symptom: Leeks have turned khaki and mushy
items.fix: Leeks need only 10 minutes; add them right at the end so they stay green and retain some texture

items.symptom: Potatoes have disintegrated into the broth
items.cause: Cooked too long or cut too small
items.fix: Halve the potatoes rather than dicing; add them for the final 30 minutes only and watch the time

items.symptom: Meat is still chewy at 90 minutes
items.cause: Neck chops vary in size; older animals take longer
items.fix: Continue simmering until the meat yields easily when pressed with a spoon; up to 2 hours is not unusual for large neck pieces

content.text: Cawl is Wales's oldest and most enduring dish, eaten in some form since the medieval period. The leek (Wales's national emblem) gives the dish its most distinctive characteristic, though the vegetable list has always varied with the season and what the farm produced. For many Welsh families, cawl is the food of Saints' Day, of cold Sundays, and of the week after a roast when the bones go back into the pot. The caerphilly on the side is not a garnish; it is eaten between spoonfuls, the sharpness cutting through the fatty broth.
